61496e7ef852cdd1c8d65d5e1a2459a4ba1b6f3e.text: Panic! There is no big deal with panic. It is memory-safe. Similarly to abort, std::terminate, __builtin_trap, or nullptr dereference in C++ But Rust libraries tend to panic too much: • It could be an indication of a bug (failed assertion) - then the library should benefit from better testing and fuzzing, which is must have in C++ but less so in Rust • It could be mistakenly used instead of exceptions (which Rust don't favor) and it's ok to terminate on panic in batch applications, but not ok in server and interactive applications ClickHouse CI system found bugs in each and every Rust library that we used (to be honest it also found bugs in almost every C and C++ library) 14 ClickHouse

When you use sanitizers in C++, all code must be compiled with sanitizers, including the code in Rust. For example, MSan requires every memory write to be instrumented, and ASan requires instrumenting every memory allocation/deallocation. This is another reason for compiling everything from source. Sanitizers in Rust were a problem in 2022, but in 2025 it is as easy as specifying the right toolchain for Rust. 16 ||||| ClickHouse

aae8013e8b7b281fe485a128ae4e4dee75ff508a.text: Prototyping new features Cache for deserialized columns #82335 Open 1 #96844 [WIP] Columns Cache #96844 Draft alexey-milovdov wants to merge 57 commits into master from columns-cache alexey-milovdov opened on Jun 21, 2025 · edited by alexey-milovdov Edits Member Conversation 11 Commits 57 Checks 137 Files changed 93 Company or project name ClickHouse Use case Goals: Make MergeTree performance no less than that of Memory tables. Provide the first step towards the cache for intermediate data in the query pipeline. Reading data from a MergeTree table requires: reading compressed data from the virtual filesystem or page cache (unless we use uncompressed cache); by the way, reading from the Linux page cache does a memory copy from the kernel to userspace (unless we use userspace page cache); decompressing this data (unless we use uncompressed cache); deserializing it into columns, which involves a memory copy or a slightly more complex operation (for ColumnString) (unless the block sizes align nicely so that the readAll method is used, but it is rare); With the columns cache, we can eliminate all these steps for hot data. Describe the solution you'd like The cache has the following structure: Table UUID -> data part -> column name -> mark range -> ColumnPtr Add settings use_columns_cache , enable_writes_to_columns_cache , enable_reads_from_columns_cache , column_cache_max_size_in_bytes , column_cache_max_entries , min_time_to_query_cache . When we read certain columns in certain mark ranges in data parts, if enable_reads_from_columns_cache is set, we first locate intersecting mark ranges in the cache. If they were found, we take them from the cache, and if necessary, cut to adjust to the requested mark range. Ranges not presented in the cache will be read as usual. The max_block_size setting is ignored in favor of returning the cached data as is. alexey-milovdov commented 5 days ago Member alexey-milovdov 1 Changelog category (leave one): New Feature Changelog entry (a user-readable short description of the changes that goes into CHANGELOG.md): Implement the deserialized columns cache that keeps columns in memory. In comparison to the uncompressed cache, this saves time on deserialization and copying. Closes #82335. e6370eb205768b87a34ca1e493d024b7b58193a4.text: Code reviews Make a thorough review of this PR: https://github.com/ClickHouse/ClickHouse/pull/48941, especially about potentially broken invariants and race conditions. Merge it with the master branch. You can commit and push directly to the author's branch. Issues Found 1. Race condition in clearOldMutations (Bug) The new code calls getUnfinishedMutationCommands() outside the lock, then uses the result inside the lock: std::map<std::string, MutationCommands> unfinished_mutations = getUnfinishedMutationCommands(); // acquires+releases lock // ... window for race ... { std::lock_guard lock(currently_processing_in_background_mutex); // re-acquires lock // uses stale unfinished_mutations } The old code called getMinPartDataVersion() inside the lock, which was safe. In the new code, between the two lock acquisitions, a concurrent ATTACH PARTITION or REPLACE PARTITION could introduce parts with old data versions, making a previously-finished mutation unfinished again. If the state unfinished_mutations snapshot doesn't reflect this, the mutation could be incorrectly cleaned up, and never be mutated. Fix: Move the logic that determines "finished" status inside the lock. Since getDataPartsVectorForInternalUsage() while holding currently_processing_in_background_mutex is already done in selectPartsToMutate, there's no deadlock risk. 2. MergeTreeMutationEntry now depends on StorageMergeTree* (Design concern) The loading constructor MergeTreeMutationEntry(disk, path, name, storage, context) tightly couples the mutation entry to the storage. This was previously a plain data-only struct loadable from disk. The new constructor calls storage_->getPartitionIdsAffectedByCommands(...) which evaluates partition expressions. While this works (partition key definitions are immutable in MergeTree), it's an unclean layering inversion - a data structure in MergeTree/ now depends on StorageMergeTree. The forward declaration in the header and #include <Storages/StorageMergeTree.h> in the cpp manager both add fragility. If MergeTreeMutationEntry is ever used by StorageReplicatedMergeTree for for ClickHouse

e73680fd4d15b30e20e851e4e438f4a84b1eaee7.text: Problems with Rust Rust might be perfect. But a combination of Rust and C++ is asking for trouble... Reproducible builds: • all dependencies must be pinned to avoid supply chain attacks • they must be built from the source code • the build system cannot download anything from the Internet This is solved in our C++ codebase. And I believe it is non-issue in Rust. But it took a long way to ensure hermetic builds using corrosion in CMake. (The solution is trivial – vendoring the dependencies). 11

effd1899b80ea84612fb5446d72ee9ff9258c3b8.text: Design For Production Workloads There are many database projects designed to work in specific environments: - FPGA databases; GPU databases; - databases for zoned SSDs; programmable SSDs; - databases requiring RDMA over Infiniband; - databases relying on 100% reliable network; - hardware transactional memory and persistent memory; - high socket-count systems, mainframes... They don't survive. A good database must scale on common hardware. ClickHouse works on x86_64, AArch64, RISC-V; Linux, Mac, and FreeBSD. ClickHouse
